narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and then hearing you'll likely struggle with the bar exam feels like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for the handle. Our prediction model looked at your profile and saw a 66% chance of not passing the bar, painting a picture of someone who's had a tough academic climb so far, even with your solid background. The biggest red flag for it was your Year 3 decile of 1, meaning you ranked at the very bottom of your class that year, which strongly signaled potential ongoing challenges ahead. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2, showing early struggles that set a pattern of needing to fight harder just to keep up. Your LSAT score of 30 added to the worry, as it's on the lower side and often a key predictor of bar success in patterns we've seen. Even your upper-middle family income, while helpful for support, didn't outweigh these academic hurdles in the model's eyes—it sometimes correlates with higher pressure or mismatched preparation paths. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.5 is respectable but couldn't fully counter the downward law school trend. Factors like being white, planning full-time studies, and identifying as female all nudged the prediction toward risk in subtle ways, based on historical outcomes. Together, these painted you as a determined fighter from a good home, but one whose grades have consistently lagged in competitive settings, making the bar's intense demands feel like too steep a hill. This isn't about your worth—it's a data-driven caution to build those academic muscles stronger before diving in. Many in similar spots have turned it around with targeted prep; maybe that's your next chapter. We're rooting for you to rewrite this story.
